questions.question: What problem with Transfusion's two-loss training does Show-o sidestep?
options: Transfusion cannot generate text

The continuous diffusion loss lives on a different numerical scale from discrete NTP, making loss-weight balancing a hyperparameter search — Show-o keeps BOTH modalities discrete and uses one masked-token-prediction objective
Transfusion requires two GPUs minimum
Transfusion's license forbids commercial use
questions.explanation: Show-o's move: discrete like Chameleon, but generate images in PARALLEL via masked discrete diffusion instead of token-by-token — a single loss formulation that generalizes next-token prediction.

questions.question: How does MaskGIT-style masked discrete diffusion generate an image?
options: It denoises continuous latents over 1000 steps
Start fully masked, predict ALL masked tokens in parallel each step, keep the top-K most confident, re-mask the rest — after ~8-16 iterations every token is filled in
It autoregressively emits one token per forward pass
It copies the nearest training image
questions.explanation: Training is BERT for images: sample a masking ratio uniformly from [0,1], mask that fraction of the VQ tokens, train the transformer to recover them. The per-step unmasking schedule (cosine works well) is tuned.

questions.question: How does Show-o's unified loss cover both text generation and image generation?
options: It uses MSE for both modalities
Cross-entropy on <MASK> tokens generalizes both: text NTP is the special case where only the last token is "masked", image generation masks a random subset — one formulation, two masking patterns
Two optimizers with synced learning rates
It doesn't — text and image use unrelated losses
questions.explanation: Training alternates standard NTP on text, masked-token prediction for text-to-image, and VQA samples (image → text, really just NTP). The hybrid attention mask is causal for text, bidirectional within the image block.

questions.question: How do the three unified-generation approaches compare on image-decoding cost?
options: All three need the same number of forward passes

Chameleon/Emu3 autoregress over 1024-4096 tokens (one pass each); Transfusion runs ~20 continuous-diffusion steps; Show-o needs ~16 parallel masked-prediction steps with cheaper per-step discrete logits
Show-o is the slowest of the three
Transfusion generates in a single pass
questions.explanation: Parallel decoding is the speed story: ~16 full-transformer passes versus thousands of per-token passes makes Show-o much faster than Chameleon at similar scale, roughly matching Transfusion's step count at lower per-step cost.

questions.question: Why can one Show-o checkpoint serve T2I, VQA, and inpainting?
options: Three LoRA adapters are swapped at runtime
Task selection is just prompt format — masked-token prediction with the hybrid mask covers them all; inpainting is image generation where the known region's tokens start unmasked
A classifier routes to three hidden experts
It can't — each task needs a fine-tune
questions.explanation: Everything is "recover the masked tokens given the visible ones." VQA masks text positions, T2I masks image positions, inpainting masks only the region to fill — one backbone, one objective.
